did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed DermAI, a hybrid deep learning framework using EfficientNet-B4 for visual feature extraction from dermoscopic images and fusing them with patient metadata (age, gender, lesion location) to perform automated skin cancer classification (benign vs. malignant) on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing 'DermAI', a hybrid deep learning framework for skin cancer classification. It details the methodology (EfficientNet-B4, metadata fusion), dataset (HAM10000), training protocols, and reports new empirical results (85% accuracy, 0.9243 ROC-AUC). Although published in conference proceedings (ICISS-2026), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: DermAI: Automatic Skin Cancer Classification using Deep Learning
journal: Proceedings of the 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS-2026)
brief_summary: This study presents DermAI, a hybrid deep learning framework for automated skin cancer classification that fuses dermoscopic image features with patient metadata such as age, gender, and lesion location. The system utilizes EfficientNet-B4 for visual feature extraction and integrates these with clinical context to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, employing techniques like stratified data balancing and class weighting to address class imbalance. Evaluation results demonstrated an accuracy of 85% and a ROC-AUC of 0.9243, outperforming conventional transfer learning baselines. The framework includes a Flask-based backend API and web interface to facilitate real-time inference and clinical integration. Ultimately, DermAI aims to provide a scalable, interpretable, and accessible tool for early skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.details: The model performs binary skin cancer classification by predicting the probability of malignancy using a sigmoid output layer. It distinguishes between benign lesions (Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma) and malignant lesions (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) aggregated from the HAM10000 dataset. Evaluation metrics include accuracy (85%), precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC (0.9243).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large, publicly available collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images obtained from the Kaggle web portal, containing verified images labeled by medical experts. It includes over 10,000 high-quality images representing seven common pigmented skin lesion classes.

use.training: Used for training the DermAI framework after augmentation and balancing (targeting ~4,000 malignant and ~4,000 benign samples).
use.validation: Used for validation via stratified split (15% of dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing via stratified split (15% of dataset).
use.other: Original diagnostic categories were mapped to two binary classes: Malignant and Benign.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance and emphasize the minority class during training.
To ensure representative distribution of classes in training, validation, and testing sets.
To evaluate the contribution of specific model components (image-only, metadata-only, augmentation, fine-tuning strategy) to overall performance.
To evaluate model performance across classes, accounting for class imbalance.
To analyze true positive, false positive, true negative, and false negative counts for detailed performance assessment.
To ensure reproducibility of experiments and mitigate non-determinism in training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, ROC-AUC, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices, with comparisons made via ablation studies and descriptive metrics rather than statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment: DermAI (Hybrid Deep Learning Framework: EfficientNet-B4 + Metadata Fusion)

DermAI (Ablation: Image-only).Note: Drop in macro-F1 by 3–4% compared to full model

DermAI (Ablation: Metadata-only).Note: Substantially lower performance (AUC near random for some splits)

DermAI (Ablation: Top-layer vs Mid-layer.Note: Improved AUC when mid-layers were unfrozen carefully compared to only top-layer fine-tuning

HAM10000.notes: The model outperformed conventional transfer learning baselines. The study highlights that the fusion of image features and patient metadata (age, gender, lesion location) improved diagnostic precision. Class imbalance was addressed via class weighting (inverse class frequency) and augmentation (targeting ~4,000 malignant and ~4,000 benign samples in training).

architecture_used.main_model: DermAI (Hybrid Deep Learning Framework combining EfficientNet-B4 and Metadata Fusion)
architecture_used.input_size: Image: 380x380x3; Metadata: Vector of length M (age, gender, lesion location)

image_branch.feature_extraction: GlobalAveragePooling2D applied to obtain image feature vector
image_branch.fine_tuning_strategy: Staged unfreezing: Top 30 layers unfrozen first, then mid-layers (e.g., layers 40-70) unfrozen

metadata_branch.structure: 2-3 Dense layers with Batch Normalization and Dropout

architecture_details.fusion_mechanism: Concatenation of image feature vector and metadata embedding
architecture_details.fusion_head: Two Dense layers (256 and 64 units) with Dropout and Batch Normalization

regularization: L2 weight decay on dense layers

preprocessing_techniques: Min-max normalization of pixel values to [0,1]
Image augmentation (random rotations ±30 degrees, width/height shifts up to 10%, zoom range 90%-110%, horizontal/vertical flips, brightness adjustments ±20%)
Stratified data splitting (70% train, 15% validation, 15% test)
Class balancing via oversampling of malignant images to target ~4,000 samples
Metadata cleaning (replacing spaces in localization with underscores)
Metadata encoding (gender converted to numeric, localization one-hot encoded and converted to integer, age standardized using StandardScaler)
Handling missing values (median imputation for missing ages per binary class, explicit unknown category for missing localization)
tf.data pipeline operations (shuffle, prefetch, parallel map for decoding/augmentation)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to malignant images with a range of ±30 degrees.
Applied to malignant images with shifts up to 10%.
Applied to malignant images with a range of 90%–110%.
Applied to malignant images with adjustments of ±20%.
Augmentation was applied only to malignant images until a target of approximately 4,000 malignant samples was reached to balance the class distribution.

conclusions: The proposed DermAI hybrid deep learning framework, which fuses EfficientNet-B4 visual features with patient metadata (age, gender, lesion location), achieved an accuracy of 85% and a ROC-AUC of 0.9243 on the HAM10000 dataset.
The fusion of clinical metadata with dermoscopic image features improved diagnostic precision and outperformed conventional transfer learning baselines.
Ablation studies confirmed that metadata fusion is critical, as image-only models showed a 3–4% drop in macro-F1 and metadata-only models performed near random chance.
Staged fine-tuning, specifically unfreezing mid-level convolutional layers (e.g., layers 40–70) in addition to top layers, improved ROC-AUC compared to fine-tuning only the top layers.

The model demonstrated high sensitivity for malignant lesions (Recall: 0.95) and high precision for benign lesions (Precision: 0.97), balancing the trade-off between sensitivity and precision despite class imbalance.
Class imbalance was effectively managed through targeted augmentation of malignant images and inverse class weighting strategies.
The system includes a Flask-based backend API and web interface to facilitate real-time inference and clinical integration.

Limitations acknowledged include dataset bias inherent to the HAM10000 population characteristics, potential imaging artifacts, and the necessity for broader validation across diverse populations and prospective trials before clinical deployment.
The study positions DermAI as a clinical decision support tool rather than a replacement for professional medical judgment.
important_remarks: The study is limited to the HAM10000 dataset, which introduces population bias inherent to its specific demographic characteristics; external validation on diverse populations is required before clinical deployment.
The paper acknowledges that the system is intended as a clinical decision support tool and not a replacement for professional medical judgment.

Performance metrics (85% accuracy, 0.9243 ROC-AUC) were derived from a single stratified split (70% train, 15% validation, 15% test) of the HAM10000 dataset, lacking multi-center or prospective trial validation.
Class imbalance was addressed via oversampling of malignant images and inverse class weighting, which may not fully reflect real-world prevalence distributions.

The model relies on patient metadata (age, gender, lesion location) which, if missing or inaccurate in clinical settings, could degrade performance; the paper notes handling of missing values but does not report performance under high missingness rates.
Potential imaging artifacts and variations in dermoscopic image acquisition quality are noted as limitations affecting generalizability.
The paper reports no formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to compare DermAI against baselines, relying instead on descriptive metrics and ablation studies.
Implementation details note non-determinism due to cuDNN optimizations, with results averaged across multiple runs, though specific variance or confidence intervals are not provided.
